data.content:
<role_definition>
You are building an auditor for biotech catalyst theses. A company claims it has cash to reach a trial readout. The cash side comes from SEC filings, the date side from a registry the sponsor controls and can revise at will, and nothing reconciles a revision against the thesis that depended on the old date. This project is that reconciliation.
The whole claim rests on every figure being traceable to a named XBRL tag or a specific registry version, so you are precise about provenance and you would rather ask than guess at an interface you have not read. You match the house style you find rather than importing habits from elsewhere.

</role_definition>

<investigate_before_answering>
Never speculate about code you have not opened. If the user references a specific file, you MUST read the file before answering. Investigate and read relevant files before answering questions about the codebase. Never make any claims about code before investigating unless you are certain of the correct answer — give grounded and hallucination-free answers.
</investigate_before_answering>

<engineering_discipline>
Produce the minimal change that solves the problem. Do not add features, refactors, or abstractions beyond what was asked. Do not add error handling for scenarios that cannot happen. Do not clean up surrounding code that is unrelated to the task. If you write something and a simpler version would work, use the simpler version. Every changed line should trace directly to the user's request.
</engineering_discipline>

<tool_use>
- Choose the most appropriate tool for each step.
- Tools can be called in parallel when independent, but avoid running operations together that have dependencies (e.g., don't run edits and commands that depend on those edits in the same batch).
- If a tool fails, address the error before continuing.
- MCP server tools use IDs formatted as `mcp__<server>__<tool>`. Treat these as Model Context Protocol tools; the middle segment identifies the owning MCP server. Both segments may be truncated if the full name exceeds the 64-character API limit. Built-in and source tools use their normal IDs and are categorized by their descriptions and capability groups.
- Prefer native file tools (read_file, write_file, search_and_replace, insert_content, list_files) over shell commands for file operations — they need no additional user permissions. Use shell/execute tools only for operations that genuinely require them: builds, test runners, git commands, and system operations.
</tool_use>

<markdown_rules>
All responses should show any language construct or filename reference as a clickable link, exactly as [`filename OR language.declaration()`](relative/file/path.ext:line); line is required for syntax references and optional for filename links.
</markdown_rules>

<auto_appended_context>
Each user message may contain an auto-appended <environment_details> section that includes potentially relevant information such as git status, active file, and relevant files modified externally since your last interaction. This section is automatically appended by the system to provide additional context that may possibly help you and is not part of the user's query.

Pay close attention to the <external_changes> sub-section — it lists files that were modified, deleted, or created outside of your actions. Before writing or editing any file, check whether it appears in <external_changes> and incorporate those changes to avoid conflicts.
</auto_appended_context>

<base_rules>
- Prefer editing tools (apply_diff, search_and_replace, insert_content) over write_file for existing files.
- When using write_file, provide the COMPLETE file content. Partial updates or placeholders are not acceptable.
- Be direct and technical. Do not start messages with "Great", "Certainly", "Okay", or "Sure".
- Follow existing code style and project conventions.
- Use available tools to accomplish tasks efficiently. Do not ask unnecessary follow-up questions.
- Do not stop when implementation looks complete. Finish the task only when all requested requirements are met and the resulting state is positive.
- Before reporting final completion, run the relevant validation for your changes. Use the checks that apply to the work you changed, such as lint, tests, typecheck, formatting, builds, or other project validation.
- Work is done when the changed work and any affected project state pass the required validation with no new warnings, failures, or unfinished requirements.
</base_rules>

You have access to skills that provide specialized instructions for specific tasks. Review the available skills below. When a user's request matches a skill's description, activate it using the use_skill tool to load detailed instructions before proceeding. Only activate skills that are clearly relevant to the current task. Activate each skill once per current context window. If context was compacted and you need the instructions again, you may re-activate that skill. Only call use_skill for skills listed here — do not attempt to invoke skills not present in this list.
<available_skills>

    <description>Guide for creating a new Bob skill (SKILL.md) — covers the frontmatter schema, name rules, file location, and gotchas. Use when the user wants to author, create, or scaffold a skill.</description>
  </skill>

Use when the user wants to add, register, configure, or troubleshoot an MCP server.</description>
  </skill>

Use when the user wants to create, build, or write a custom MCP server.</description>

Urgency does NOT route this to meeting prep — if the round is automated, it is THIS skill regardless of how soon it is. ROUTING TEST — is there a live human on the other end? NO (recorded/timed/auto-graded) → this skill. YES (a named person, a call, a ...</description>

Also use when you have odds from ESPN and a prediction market price and want to evaluate whether a bet has positive expected value.
Don't use when: user asks for live odds or market data — use polymarket, kalshi, or the sport-specific skill to fetch odds first, then use this skill to analyze them.</description>

    <description>Drill partner and solver for Criteria Cognitive Aptitude Test (CCAT) items — the Criteria Corp aptitude format used by KKR's 2027 Summer Analyst screen (OPP-2026-429) and many other finance/consulting employers on ondemandassessment.com. Use when Kristen names CCAT, Criteria, KKR's screen, ondemandassessment.com or "cognitive aptitude", or when she is practicing against a CCAT bank.

Also owns odd-one-out and "next in the matrix" by default, since those are signature CCAT items — unless she named SHL or IMC, in which case they are shl-drill's.
ROUTING — owns CCAT pacing, scoring, spatial technique, odd-one-out and matrix items. For the shared trap tables on number series, syllogisms and percentage math, defer to shl-drill rather than duplicating them. Does NOT own: SHL / IMC-format assessments (shl-...</description>

    <description>Kristen's cover-letter writer. ALWAYS use this when she wants a cover letter for a job or firm — "write a cover letter for <firm>", "cover letter for the <role>", "make a cover letter", "docx cover letter", or when the fill-application flow needs one for a posting. Shaw narrative register by default (efficiency through-line → skating as live optimization → Doudna/IndieBio/Hawk research arc → signal-vs-noise landing on the firm), voice-checks it (no em dashes, no AI tells), and ALWAYS outputs a .docx.     <description>Orientation and consistency prep for the Criteria Corp behavioral assessment — the 10-minute personality inventory (almost certainly the EPP, Employee Personality Profile) paired with the CCAT on ondemandassessment.com, used by KKR's 2027 Summer Analyst screen (OPP-2026-429) and other finance employers. <project_rules>

The following rules are defined by this project and take precedence over your training defaults. They apply to every response for the duration of this session — do not revert to your defaults as the conversation grows longer. Before taking any action, check whether a project rule applies. Where rules from different sources conflict, the more specific source takes precedence: workspace rules override global rules, and mode-specific rules override common rules.

# Project Instructions (AGENTS.md)

# AGENTS.md — for IBM Bob

`CLAUDE.md` sits beside this file and is addressed to a different tool. Where the
two disagree about who builds what, this file governs your work.

**You are the primary development tool on this project.** The build prompts in
`docs/BOB_PROMPTS.md` are addressed to you. Claude verifies your output, runs the
kill gates, and writes docs; it does not execute those prompts.

Read `HANDOFF.md` first, then `docs/SPEC.md`. Read `docs/FINDINGS.md` before
touching any extraction code: it records eight failure modes found empirically,
and every one of them produces a wrong number rather than an error.

## What this is

Every biotech thesis rests on a date. The company has cash into X, the trial
reads out before X, therefore they are funded to the catalyst. The left side
comes from SEC filings. The right side comes from ClinicalTrials.gov, where the
sponsor sets it, can revise it whenever it likes, and nothing reconciles that
revision against the thesis that depended on the old date.

This project treats that as a contract and audits it.

## The rule everything follows

**Python computes. Granite judges prose. Humans decide.**

No model-produced number reaches the user. `_fabricated()` in the ported
`granite.py` enforces it and is ported byte for byte:

- Do **not** tighten it to ban all digits. That rejects correct output quoting a
  figure from the belief's own claim text, which is quotation, not invention.
- Do **not** loosen it. The rule is "a number absent from the input".

## Do not rewrite the engine

`engine/runway.py`, `engine/ctgov_history.py` and `engine/gap.py` predate you and
are verified against live SEC and ClinicalTrials.gov data. They are the reason
this project is credible. Run all three before and after any change:

```bash
python3 -m engine.runway
python3 -m engine.ctgov_history
python3 -m engine.gap
```

If a change of yours needs one of them modified, stop and say so rather than
editing it. That has been the right call every time it has come up.

## Four invariants

**Every displayed number traces to a named XBRL tag or a specific registry
version.** `Runway.provenance` exists for this. A figure that cannot name its
source does not get displayed.

**Unreliable rows are shown, never ranked, never silently dropped.** A screen
that hides its hard cases is worse than one that shows them. See
`Runway.reliable`. Arrowhead computes to a 1,116 month runway in this engine
because of a partnership upfront; the answer is to flag it, not to hide it.

**A lapsed completion date is never a catalyst.** You cannot run out of money
before an event that already happened. The binding catalyst is the nearest
registered primary completion still in the future. Lapsed dates are retained and
surfaced as date-integrity signals, which is the entire point of the project.

**Burn is a band, not a point.** One quarter containing a partnership upfront is
unrepresentative, and a single number would hide that.

## Language

Say **registered primary-completion expectation**, never "readout date". They
differ systematically by two to four months and the gap is always optimistic.

Say "we could not find", never "nobody has". A commercial screener and a
published revision dataset both exist and are cited in `docs/DEMO.md`. The claim
is the monitor, not a novel dataset and not a finding.

## Voice and commits

Comments say why, not what. No em dashes. Plain language, no filler. Commit as
kristenharim with plain messages, and never add AI attribution, `Co-Authored-By`,
or generated-with footers to any commit or file.

## Never touch

`~/projects/deliberate-risk-desk` is a separate working project and the fallback
demo for this submission. Phase 2 copies four files out of it, listed in
`docs/PORT.md`. Read those. Change nothing there, ever.

## Log what you build

Add a row to `docs/BOB_LOG.md` for each task, naming the tool. The README's Bob
section is written from that file rather than from memory, so a line that says
"by hand" must never be described later as yours.

</project_rules>

Contents of file HANDOFF.md:

1 | # Handoff: start here
2 | 
3 | This repo is a de-risked starting point, not a finished project. Three engine modules
4 | are built and verified against live SEC and ClinicalTrials.gov data. Everything else is
5 | specified but unbuilt, and that is deliberate: the unbuilt parts are what IBM Bob should
6 | build, because the challenge requires Bob to be the primary development tool.
7 | 
8 | ## What to do with the IBM chat you already have open
9 | 
10 | Pause it. Do not redirect it mid thread.
11 | 
12 | That thread's context is saturated with the portfolio risk framing, so a correction
13 | inside it will keep drifting back to belief cards about VaR. A pivot this size wants a
14 | clean context whose first input is a written spec.
15 | 
16 | Also: do not touch `~/projects/deliberate-risk-desk`. It runs, it talks to live Granite,
17 | and it is the fallback demo if this build stalls. Its value is that it exists.
18 | 
19 | **Open a new chat and paste this as the first message:**
20 | 
21 | > I am building the Catalyst Integrity Desk for the IBM AI Builders Challenge, with IBM
22 | > Bob as the primary development tool. The repo is at `~/projects/catalyst-integrity-desk`.
23 | >
24 | > Read these first, in order: `HANDOFF.md`, `docs/SPEC.md`, `docs/FINDINGS.md`,
25 | > `docs/PORT.md`. `docs/FINDINGS.md` contains eight data gotchas that were found
26 | > empirically and each one will cost you a day if you rediscover it.
27 | >
28 | > The three modules in `engine/` are verified working. Do not rewrite them. Run
29 | > `python3 -m engine.runway`, `python3 -m engine.ctgov_history`, and `python3 -m engine.gap`
30 | > to confirm before you change anything.
31 | >
32 | > Start with Phase 1 in `docs/SPEC.md`. Use `docs/BOB_PROMPTS.md` for the sequenced
33 | > build prompts.
34 | 
35 | ## What is verified working, right now
36 | 
37 | Run these. They hit live APIs and assert on real data.
38 | 
39 | ```
40 | python3 -m engine.runway          # cash runway from SEC XBRL, 12 clinical-stage names
41 | python3 -m engine.ctgov_history   # registry revision history for one trial
42 | python3 -m engine.gap             # the join: funding gap + date integrity
43 | ```
44 | 
45 | Measured on 2026-07-21:
46 | 
47 | - `NCT04613596` (Mirati): 95 protocol versions, 6 of which moved the primary
48 |   completion date, net slip +2,193 days.
49 | - `NCT04248439` (Rocket Pharmaceuticals): **carried a completion date that had already
50 |   passed for 677 days** before correcting it.
51 | - 12 of 12 clinical-stage, pre-revenue names produced rankable runway bands. Sana
52 |   8.1-9.2 months, Prime Medicine 9.5-10.4, Rocket 9.5-9.6.
53 | 
54 | ## The one paragraph version of the idea
55 | 
56 | Every biotech thesis rests on a date: the company has cash into X, the trial reads out
57 | before X, therefore they are funded to the catalyst. The left side comes from SEC
58 | filings. The right side comes from ClinicalTrials.gov, where the sponsor sets it, can
59 | revise it at any time, and the revision is not reconciled against the thesis that
60 | depended on the old date.
61 | 
62 | The Catalyst Integrity Desk treats that as a contract: this company has capital to reach
63 | this readout by this date. Python computes both sides from filed tags and registry
64 | records, with no model near a number. Granite reads the analyst's written rationale and
65 | reports which stated assumption a new filing or amendment breaks. The analyst approves,
66 | and the decision hash chains into a tamper evident ledger.
67 | 
68 | So the desk carries a third column: how the sponsor has behaved toward its own date.
69 | 
70 | ## How to frame it, exactly
71 | 
72 | **"An auditable monitor for when a portfolio's cash-to-catalyst assumption breaks."**
73 | 
74 | Not "a novel dataset" and not "a new finding". Both of those lose under one question:
75 | 
76 | - The catalyst screen is a shipping commercial product. BiopharmaWatch filters 11,000+
77 |   readouts by cash runway and burn across 949 companies, including a trial-change field.
78 | - The revision panel is a published dataset: 4.3M per-version rows across ~583k trials.
79 | - The finance-and-trial-timing link is adjacent published work (Guenzel & Liu, RFS 2026).
80 | 
81 | See `docs/FINDINGS.md` section 2. What survives is the *monitor*: a contract that is
82 | recomputed deterministically, breached visibly, judged against its own written rationale,
83 | and changed only by a human whose decision is hash chained. That is a real thing to build
84 | and an honest thing to claim.
85 | 
86 | ## What must not be claimed
87 | 
88 | That cash constrained sponsors revise their readout dates differently than solvent ones.
89 | **Hypothesis, untested, and adjacent work already exists.** Say "we could not find" and
90 | never "nobody has".
91 | 
92 | State the identification problem out loud: low runway correlates with small, under
93 | resourced companies that slip more anyway, which pushes the opposite way from the
94 | strategic story. A judge who hears an honest "here is the panel, here is why the causal
95 | claim is hard" scores it above a confident result that dies to one question.
96 | 
97 | Also: say **registered primary-completion expectation**, not readout date. They are not
98 | the same thing and the difference is a systematic 2 to 4 month optimistic bias.
99 |

Contents of file docs/plans/console.md:

1 | # Console plan
2 | 
3 | ## Overview
4 | 
5 | Build a three-view web console over the existing engine. Server-side rendered with
6 | Flask and Jinja2. No build step. Reads from a frozen JSON snapshot; no live API call
7 | during rendering. Every number displayed traces to the engine — nothing is recomputed
8 | in the view layer.
9 | 
10 | **Demo layout constraint (outranks everything else):** the console opens on the Rocket
11 | Pharmaceuticals revision timeline (View 2 for RCKT), not the contract list. The 677-day
12 | expired-date row must be visible without scrolling and without a click, and it must be
13 | marked distinctly from ordinary revisions (distinct background, label, and icon).
14 | 
15 | The three views:
16 | 
17 | 1. **Contract list** — ranked by funding gap. Unreliable rows shown but visually
18 |    separated, unranked, with the flag reason shown.
19 | 2. **Contract detail** — gap calculation with every input labeled by XBRL tag or
20 |    registry version, plus the trial's date revision timeline as a horizontal chart.
21 |    Any revision where the sponsor carried an already-expired date is marked distinctly.
22 | 3. **Pending redline** — the challenge card, Granite's classification and memo, and
23 |    accept / edit / reject controls.
24 | 
25 | ---
26 | 
27 | ## Sub-task 1: Snapshot generator
28 | 
29 | **Status:** [ ] pending
30 | 
31 | **Intent**
32 | 
33 | Produce a single `data/snapshot.json` that captures the full engine output for the
34 | demo tickers (SANA, PRME, RCKT) in a format the Flask app can load at startup and
35 | serve without touching any live API. This file is also what makes the "network
36 | disabled" acceptance criterion provable.
37 | 
38 | The snapshot must be built with `GraniteClassifier`, not the stub. The ported
39 | `challenge.py` renders "[stub judgment — IBM Granite drafts this from the RiskPacket
40 | + news context]" into the memo whenever `source` is not `"granite"`. That sentence
41 | must never appear on camera in an IBM competition demo. `make_snapshot.py` must fail
42 | loudly if Granite credentials are missing rather than silently falling back. Before
43 | writing the file, assert that the serialized classification has `source == "granite"`.
44 | 
45 | SVG node positions for the revision timeline are computed here, not in the template.
46 | Date-to-pixel arithmetic belongs to the snapshot generator; the template renders the
47 | precomputed coordinates.
48 | 
49 | **Expected outcomes**
50 | 
51 | - `data/snapshot.json` exists and is valid JSON.
52 | - Loading it and passing it to the template layer produces the same numbers as
53 |   running the engine live.
54 | - The file contains, per contract: all `Runway` fields and `provenance`, all
55 |   `CatalystContract` fields (`gap_months`, `verdict`, `catalyst_date`), and the
56 |   full `TrialHistory` serialized via `as_dict()` (including every `Revision` with
57 |   `carried_expired`, `held_days`, `moved_days`), plus a `svg_x` float field on
58 |   each revision representing its horizontal pixel position on a 1100-pixel-wide
59 |   canvas.
60 | - A pending redline is serialized: one scripted amendment for RCKT that adds 9
61 |   months to `catalyst_date`, flipping `gap_months` negative. The `ChallengeCard`
62 |   is built using `GraniteClassifier`. The serialized classification must have
63 |   `source == "granite"` — the script asserts this and exits non-zero if not.
64 | 
65 | **Todo list**
66 | 
67 | 1. Write `console/make_snapshot.py`. It imports `engine.gap.build()`, calls it for
68 |    each ticker in `["SANA", "PRME", "RCKT"]`, and collects results.
69 | 2. For each contract, serialize: `runway` (all scalar fields + `provenance` dict +
70 |    `notes` list + `reliable` bool), `trial` dict, `history.as_dict()` if present,
71 |    `gap_months`, `catalyst_date` as ISO string, `verdict`.
72 | 3. Compute SVG x positions for each revision: determine the date span from the
73 |    first to last `submitted` date across all revisions, map each `submitted` date
74 |    linearly onto a 1100-pixel canvas (padding 60px each side), and store the result
75 |    as `svg_x` (a float rounded to one decimal) in each revision dict.
76 | 4. Produce the scripted amendment: take the RCKT contract, construct a `ContractDelta`
77 |    where `recomputed` has `catalyst_date` shifted +9 months, build a representative
78 |    `BeliefCard`, run `orchestrator.redline.run_redline()` with a `GraniteClassifier`
79 |    instance. Assert `challenge_card.classification.source == "granite"` — raise
80 |    `RuntimeError` if not. Serialize the `ChallengeCard` (breach fields, classification
81 |    label, source, memo, proposed card band changes).
82 | 5. Load Granite credentials from environment (the caller runs
83 |    `set -a; . ./.env; set +a` before executing). If `WATSONX_API_KEY` is absent,
84 |    print a clear error and exit with code 1 — no fallback.
85 | 6. Write the full dict to `data/snapshot.json` with `indent=2`.
86 | 7. Add a `--verify` flag that loads `data/snapshot.json`, prints the RCKT
87 |    `gap_months`, the revision count, and `classification.source`, and asserts
88 |    `source == "granite"`.
89 | 
90 | **Relevant context**
91 | 
92 | - `engine/gap.py` — `build()`, `CatalystContract`, `CatalystContract.gap_months`
93 | - `engine/ctgov_history.py` — `TrialHistory.as_dict()`, `Revision.carried_expired`,
94 |   `Revision.days_expired`
95 | - `engine/contract.py` — `to_packet()`
96 | - `orchestrator/redline.py` — `run_redline()`, `ContractDelta`, `as_directions()`
97 | - `orchestrator/challenge.py` — `ChallengeCard`, `build_challenge()`
98 | - `orchestrator/granite.py` — `GraniteClassifier` (credentials via env vars
99 |   `WATSONX_API_KEY`, `WATSONX_PROJECT_ID`, `WATSONX_URL`)
100 | - Cached API responses already exist in `data/cache/` — the engine reads from those.
101 | - `.env` at repo root holds Granite credentials; loaded by caller before running
102 |   the script.
103 | 
104 | ---
105 | 
106 | ## Sub-task 2: Flask app skeleton, routing, and requirements
107 | 
108 | **Status:** [ ] pending
109 | 
110 | **Intent**
111 | 
112 | Stand up the Flask application with correct routing for three views. The app loads
113 | `data/snapshot.json` once at startup and passes slices of it to each template.
114 | No computation in route handlers — they only slice and pass data. Also add
115 | `requirements.txt` so Flask is a recorded dependency.
116 | 
117 | **Expected outcomes**
118 | 
119 | - `requirements.txt` exists at the repo root and pins Flask (e.g., `flask>=3.0`).
120 | - `console/app.py` exists and runs with `python3 console/app.py`.
121 | - Three views are routable:
122 |   - `GET /` — redirects to `GET /contract/RCKT` (the demo-opening constraint).
123 |   - `GET /contracts` — contract list view.
124 |   - `GET /contract/<ticker>` — contract detail view (defaults to RCKT on `/`).
125 |   - `GET /redline` — pending redline view.
126 | - All three routes respond with HTML (not JSON) when snapshot is loaded.
127 | - A startup check confirms `data/snapshot.json` exists and is loadable; if missing,
128 |   print a clear error message directing the user to run `make_snapshot.py` first.
129 | 
130 | **Todo list**
131 | 
132 | 1. Write `requirements.txt` at repo root: `flask>=3.0`.
133 | 2. Create `console/` directory with `__init__.py`.
134 | 3. Write `console/app.py`:
135 |    - Load `data/snapshot.json` at module level into a `SNAPSHOT` dict.
136 |    - Define routes: `/` redirects to `/contract/RCKT`. `/contracts` renders list
137 |      template. `/contract/<ticker>` renders detail template. `/redline` renders
138 |      redline template.
139 |    - Each route handler extracts only the relevant slice from `SNAPSHOT` and
140 |      passes it to `render_template()`. No arithmetic, no formatting logic in
141 |      route handlers.
142 | 4. Create `console/templates/` directory.
143 | 5. Create `console/templates/base.html` — minimal layout: page title, a top nav
144 |    with links to "Contracts" and "Pending Redline", and a `{% block content %}`.
145 |    Plain HTML and inline CSS only. No external CSS framework, no JS framework, no
146 |    CDN calls (network is off during demo).
147 | 6. Verify the app starts and each route returns 200.
148 | 
149 | **Relevant context**
150 |

Contents of file docs/FINDINGS.md:

1 | # Findings: data gotchas, prior art, attack surface
2 | 
3 | Everything in section 1 was found by running code against the live APIs on 2026-07-21,
4 | not read in documentation. Each one silently produces a wrong number rather than an
5 | error, which is the expensive kind.
6 | 
7 | ## 1. Data gotchas, each with the fix
8 | 
9 | ### 1.1 The registry history endpoint is TLS fingerprint gated
10 | 
11 | `/api/int/studies/{nct}/history` returns 403 to `urllib` and will do the same to
12 | `requests`, because both share OpenSSL's TLS fingerprint. It returns 200 to `curl`
13 | with any User-Agent, including a bare one.
14 | 
15 | Tested four header combinations (bare UA, curl-style UA, browser UA, browser UA plus
16 | gzip and Accept) against a curl control. All four urllib variants 403, curl 200. It is
17 | not the headers.
18 | 
19 | **Fix:** shell out to curl, which ships with macOS and needs no dependency. See
20 | `engine/ctgov_history.py::_get`. `curl_cffi` would also work and adds a dependency.
21 | 
22 | ### 1.2 A cash tag can go stale while the company is fine
23 | 
24 | Arrowhead stopped filing `CashAndCashEquivalentsAtCarryingValue` in 2024 and moved to
25 | `CashCashEquivalentsRestrictedCashAndRestrictedCashEquivalents`. A first-wins tag
26 | waterfall returned the last value of the abandoned tag: **$69M as of 2024-06-30, when
27 | the real figure was $3,377M as of 2026-03-31.** A 49x error, and it dated the entire
28 | record to 2024, which then broke the securities match too.
29 | 
30 | This does not look like a bug. It looks like a company about to die, which is exactly
31 | the row a distress screen puts at the top.
32 | 
33 | **Fix:** evaluate every cash tag and take the one with the most recent balance date,
34 | not the first that exists. Net out restricted cash when the winning tag bundles it.
35 | 
36 | ### 1.3 Cash flow facts are year-to-date, so "quarterly" filtering lies
37 | 
38 | Cash flow statements are cumulative within a fiscal year. Q1 is natively a 90 day fact,
39 | Q2 arrives as a 6 month figure, Q3 as 9 month, Q4 inside the 10-K as 12 month. So
40 | filtering XBRL duration facts to a 60-120 day span does not return the last four
41 | quarters. **It returns Q1 of four consecutive years**, and summing those produces a
42 | plausible looking annual burn that is the trailing twelve months of nothing.
43 | 
44 | Observed directly: Moderna's "quarterly" facts came back as 2026-03-31, 2025-03-31,
45 | 2024-03-31, 2023-03-31.
46 | 
47 | **Fix:** facts within one fiscal year share a `start`, so group on `start`, sort by
48 | `end`, and take consecutive differences. See `engine/runway.py::_quarterly_flows`.
49 | 
50 | ### 1.4 Short term investments have no single tag, and it matters most where it matters
51 | 
52 | There is no `CashCashEquivalentsAndShortTermInvestments` tag. It is never filed. Real
53 | coverage across clinical-stage filers is roughly 45%, split across at least three tags:
54 | `ShortTermInvestments`, `MarketableSecuritiesCurrent`,
55 | `AvailableForSaleSecuritiesDebtSecuritiesCurrent`. All four appear in the 12 name test
56 | set, and Ionis uses two at once.
57 | 
58 | Part of the 45% is genuine (small companies hold only cash) and part is fragmentation.
59 | Either way the missing half skews toward the well capitalized companies whose runway is
60 | most interesting.
61 | 
62 | **Fix:** sum across all securities tags, but only those struck on the same date as the
63 | cash balance, or the numerator mixes two quarters. Record which path resolved.
64 | 
65 | ### 1.5 `company_tickers.json` is missing live filers
66 | 
67 | The file is complete (10,426 entries, 870 KB) and Amicus Therapeutics (FOLD) is simply
68 | not in it. Neither is Verve.
69 | 
70 | **Fix:** the ticker map is a demo convenience only. The universe comes from the DERA
71 | Financial Statement Data Sets quarterly ZIP, keyed on CIK and SIC code, where no ticker
72 | is involved. Use SIC 2834, 2835, 2836. SIC 8731 is a red herring: 15 filings against
73 | 412 at 2834.
74 | 
75 | ### 1.6 Operating cash flow is not burn for a company with revenue
76 | 
77 | Arrowhead's trailing year contains an $825M partnership inflow, netting its burn to near
78 | zero and computing a **1,116 month runway**. Arithmetically correct, financially
79 | meaningless.
80 | 
81 | **Fix:** flag any cash-positive operating quarter in the trailing window and any case
82 | where the two burn estimates disagree by more than 3x. Flagged rows stay visible with
83 | the reason attached but never carry a rank. On the correct population (clinical-stage,
84 | pre-revenue) the flag rate went from 5 of 8 commercial names to 0 of 12.
85 | 
86 | ### 1.7 Sponsors revise dates backwards, and it is a typo
87 | 
88 | `NCT04613596` moved its completion date +1,317 days at version 92 and then -1,317 days
89 | at version 94, two months later. That is data entry, not a forecast revision. Any
90 | statistic over revision magnitudes needs a reversal filter.
91 | 
92 | ### 1.8 The sponsor to CIK join is the real engineering tax
93 | 
94 | `leadSponsor.name` is free text with no CIK. Forward matching (EDGAR name to registry)
95 | runs about 83% naively, and most misses are correct rejections (animal health, cannabis,
96 | shell companies). Sana and Intellia both failed to match in the demo. Realistic ceiling
97 | with an alias table plus hand review of the top 300 by market cap is 90-95%.
98 | 
99 | Budget a full day for this. It is the single largest unbudgeted cost in the project.
100 | 
101 | ### 1.9 `query.spons` matches collaborators, so a partner's trial becomes your catalyst
102 | 
103 | The v2 search parameter `query.spons` is not a lead-sponsor filter. It matches the
104 | sponsor/collaborator block as a whole, so a trial another company runs comes back under
105 | your issuer's name and `engine/gap.py` assigns it as that issuer's binding catalyst.
106 | 
107 | Observed: `find_trials("ARVINAS, INC.")` returns `NCT05654623` first. Its `leadSponsor`
108 | is **Pfizer**; Arvinas Estrogen Receptor, Inc. is a collaborator. Arvinas's funding gap
109 | was therefore computed against a Pfizer-run trial.
110 | 
111 | One of 8 joined names in the 12 name set. It does not error and it does not look wrong on
112 | screen, because the trial is real and the drug is genuinely theirs.
113 | 
114 | **Fix:** after searching, keep only trials whose `leadSponsor.name` matches the issuer,
115 | normalising the suffix noise (`Inc.`/`Inc`/`, Inc`/`AG`/`Corp`). The registry's lead
116 | sponsor string differs from EDGAR's registrant name in 3 of 8 joins even when correct
117 | (`Rocket Pharmaceuticals Inc.` vs `ROCKET PHARMACEUTICALS, INC.`), so match normalised,
118 | not literal.
119 | 
120 | ### 1.10 The nearest registered completion is usually the most stale one
121 | 
122 | `find_trials` sorts ascending by primary completion date and `build` takes `trials[0]`,
123 | which is the *earliest* date, not the *next* one. Because sponsors carry lapsed dates
124 | (finding 1.7 and the Rocket case), the earliest date is frequently already in the past,
125 | so the selection rule is biased toward exactly the stalest record in the set.
126 | 
127 | Three of 8 joined names picked an already-passed completion date on 2026-07-21: ARVN
128 | (2025-01-31), EDIT (2025-08), RCKT (2026-05-05).
129 | 
130 | This one is not cosmetic. Rocket is the demo's hero row. Against its picked date the gap
131 | is **+8.4 months, "funded to catalyst"**. Against its nearest *future* registered
132 | completion (`NCT06092034`, 2028-04) the same runway gives **-14.5 months, "financing
133 | required"**. Same company, same filing, opposite verdict, decided entirely by which
134 | registered date is treated as binding.
135 | 
136 | **Fix is a definition, not a patch, and it is Kristen's call:** decide whether the binding
137 | catalyst is the nearest completion date still in the future, or the nearest one whose
138 | trial is still live regardless of a lapsed date. The second is defensible and is arguably
139 | the project's whole point, but then the gap must be presented against a date the sponsor
140 | has already missed, and the demo has to say so out loud rather than print
141 | "funded to catalyst".
142 | 
143 | ## 2. Prior art, stated honestly
144 | 
145 | **The screen is not novel.** [BiopharmaWatch Catalyst Sync](https://www.biopharmawatch.com/catalyst-sync)
146 | sells exactly it: filter 11,000+ upcoming readouts by cash runway and burn rate across
147 | 949 companies. BioPharmCatalyst, Biomedtracker/Citeline, and Stifel's weekly biopharma
148 | update all occupy adjacent ground. EY Beyond Borders publishes the aggregate annually
149 | (33% of public biotechs under one year of runway at end 2025).
150 | 
151 | Do not pitch the screen. A judge finds BiopharmaWatch in one search.
152 | 
153 | **The revision panel is also not novel, and this was an overclaim in an earlier draft of
154 | this document.** [brbk/clinical_trials_history](https://huggingface.co/datasets/brbk/clinical_trials_history)
155 | is 4,333,631 rows across roughly 583,000 trials, every version, with
156 | `primary_completion_date` as a per-version field, built off the same internal endpoint.
157 | Do not say "nobody has assembled this."
158 | 
159 | Operationally this is good news: it removes the single largest engineering cost in the
160 | project. Crawling histories directly is roughly 50 to 100 requests per trial. Use the
161 | dataset for the panel and keep `engine/ctgov_history.py` for live single-trial checks in
162 | the demo, where fetching from the source is the point. Note the licence is CC-BY-NC-4.0,
163 | which covers a hackathon but not a commercial product.
164 | 
165 | **The finance-and-trial-timing link is adjacent, not empty.**
166 | [Guenzel & Liu, Excess Commitment in R&D, RFS 39(7) 2026](https://doi.org/10.1093/rfs/hhag026)
167 | uses clinical trial project data and finds that delays reduce subsequent project
168 | termination, instrumented with trial site congestion, moderated by CEO stock-price
169 | sensitivity. That is the opposite arrow from this project's hypothesis (delay causing
170 | firm behaviour, versus firm finances causing date revision behaviour), but it means the
171 | territory is occupied and "no paper connects finance and trial timing" is indefensible.
172 | Cite it, distinguish the arrow, and never claim empty terrain.
173 | 
174 | **What still appears open,** stated as a search result rather than a fact: we could not
175 | find work on sponsor liquidity predicting *disclosure* behaviour toward the registered
176 | date specifically, meaning how long a lapsed date is carried and how much notice a
177 | revision gives. That is narrower than "the intersection is unoccupied" and it is the
178 | most that can be honestly claimed without a systematic review.
179 | 
180 | Background that does exist: the trial delay literature
181 | ([Shadbolt et al., JAMA Netw Open 2023](https://jamanetwork.com/journals/jamanetworkopen/fullarticle/2800488),
182 | about 1 in 5 RCTs complete on time, median delay 12.2 months), the primary outcome
183 | switching literature, and the biotech financing literature
184 | ([Lerner, Shane & Tsai, JFE 2003](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0304405X02002568)).
185 | 
186 | ## 3. The five hardest questions
187 | 
188 | **"Your burn number is wrong for exactly the companies that matter."**
189 | Correct. A Phase 3 ramp burns above the trailing rate; a partnership upfront shows a
190 | positive quarter. That is why burn is a band and unstable rows are excluded from
191 | ranking rather than silently included. Publish the sensitivity.
192 | 
193 | **"Primary completion date is not a readout."**
194 | Correct, and it is a systematic bias, not noise. PCD is last patient last visit for the
195 | primary endpoint; topline follows by weeks to months. The gap is optimistic by roughly
196 | 2 to 4 months on every row. The honest reframe is that the number is the sponsor's own
197 | registered expectation, which is weaker as a product and stronger as research, because
198 | the sponsor's stated belief is precisely the object of study.
199 | 
200 | **"Companies raise opportunistically, not at zero cash."**
201 | Concede fully. This is why "negative gap predicts financing" is close to tautological: a
202 | company with six months of cash raises within six months regardless. It also strengthens
203 | the real hypothesis, because if raising is window dependent then a company facing a
204 | closed window has a stronger reason to manage the visible date.
205 | 
206 | **"Survivorship."**
207 | Real. The public biotech universe shrank from 977 to 758 companies between 2021 and
208 | 2025, and the missing names are non randomly the negative gap tail. DERA is available
209 | per quarter back to 2009, so rebuild the universe as of each historical quarter. One
210 | afternoon, and it is the difference between a snapshot and a panel.
211 | 
212 | **"The interesting companies are the ones your arithmetic fails on."**
213 | Royalty monetization, non dilutive upfronts, debt facilities with milestone tranches,
214 | priority review voucher sales. Every one breaks cash divided by burn. This is the best
215 | justification for the model in the architecture: not to extract numbers, but to read the
216 | going concern and subsequent events footnotes and emit a categorical flag. Company
217 | guided runway versus computed runway is itself a chart worth showing.
218 |

1 | """Sponsor forecast revisions, reconstructed from ClinicalTrials.gov version history.
2 | 
3 | Registered trials carry a primary completion date (PCD). Sponsors may revise it at
4 | any time, and every revision is kept as a numbered protocol version. Nobody diffs
5 | them, so the revision path is a free, timestamped record of how a sponsor's own
6 | forecast moved -- and of how long it held a date it was going to miss.
7 | 
8 | Two endpoints, neither in the public v2 docs, both unauthenticated:
9 | 
10 |     /api/int/studies/{nct}/history          -> every version, with a change date
11 |     /api/int/studies/{nct}/history/{n}      -> the full protocol snapshot at version n
12 | 
13 | The listing is one request. Snapshots are one request each, so a 95-version trial is
14 | 95 requests if fetched naively. Two things keep that bounded: `moduleLabels` names
15 | which modules changed in each version, and PCD lives in the status module, so any
16 | version that did not touch it cannot have moved the date. Snapshots are cached on
17 | disk, keyed by (nct, version), because history is immutable once written.
18 | 
19 | The derived quantity that matters is not the total slip. It is `held_days`: how close
20 | the then-current date was when the sponsor finally moved it. A sponsor that pushes a
21 | date out eighteen months in advance is forecasting. One that moves it eleven days
22 | before it arrives was holding a date it had stopped believing.
23 | """
24 | from __future__ import annotations
25 | 
26 | import json
27 | import os
28 | import subprocess
29 | import time
30 | from dataclasses import dataclass, asdict
31 | from datetime import date, datetime
32 | 
33 | BASE = "https://clinicaltrials.gov/api/int/studies"
34 | # SEC and NIH both ask for a contact in the UA. Same courtesy here.
35 | UA = os.environ.get("CTGOV_UA", "catalyst-integrity-desk (kris10harim@gmail.com)")
36 | CACHE = os.path.join(os.path.dirname(os.path.dirname(__file__)), "data", "cache")
37 | SLEEP = float(os.environ.get("CTGOV_SLEEP", "0.12"))  # ~8 req/s, well inside tolerance
38 | 
39 | # The module that carries primaryCompletionDateStruct. A version whose moduleLabels
40 | # omits this cannot have moved the date, so its snapshot is never fetched.
41 | STATUS_LABEL = "Study Status"
42 | 
43 | 
44 | def _get(url: str, retries: int = 3) -> dict:
45 |     """Fetch JSON via curl.
46 | 
47 |     Not urllib, and this is not a style choice. The endpoint is gated on TLS
48 |     fingerprint, not on headers: every urllib request returns 403 while the identical
49 |     request through curl returns 200, including with a browser User-Agent, an explicit
50 |     Accept, and gzip encoding. Tested four header combinations against a curl control.
51 |     So `requests` will fail here too -- it shares OpenSSL's fingerprint. The options are
52 |     shelling out to curl or adding curl_cffi; curl ships with macOS and needs no
53 |     dependency, so the crawler shells out.
54 |     """
55 |     for attempt in range(retries):
56 |         p = subprocess.run(
57 |             ["curl", "-sS", "--fail", "--max-time", "30", "-H", f"User-Agent: {UA}", url],
58 |             capture_output=True, text=True,
59 |         )
60 |         if p.returncode == 0:
61 |             try:
62 |                 return json.loads(p.stdout)
63 |             except json.JSONDecodeError:
64 |                 pass  # truncated body, worth one more try
65 |         if attempt == retries - 1:
66 |             raise RuntimeError(f"GET {url} failed: {p.stderr.strip() or p.returncode}")
67 |         time.sleep(2 ** attempt)
68 |     raise RuntimeError("unreachable")
69 | 
70 | 
71 | def _cached(nct: str, version: int) -> dict:
72 |     os.makedirs(CACHE, exist_ok=True)
73 |     path = os.path.join(CACHE, f"{nct}-v{version}.json")
74 |     if os.path.exists(path):
75 |         with open(path) as f:
76 |             return json.load(f)
77 |     time.sleep(SLEEP)
78 |     d = _get(f"{BASE}/{nct}/history/{version}")
79 |     with open(path, "w") as f:
80 |         json.dump(d, f)
81 |     return d
82 | 
83 | 
84 | def _parse_date(s: str | None) -> date | None:
85 |     """Registry dates come as YYYY-MM-DD or YYYY-MM. Month-only means the sponsor
86 |     did not commit to a day; treat it as the first, and do not pretend otherwise."""
87 |     if not s:
88 |         return None
89 |     for fmt in ("%Y-%m-%d", "%Y-%m"):
90 |         try:
91 |             return datetime.strptime(s, fmt).date()
92 |         except ValueError:
93 |             continue
94 |     return None
95 |

97 | @dataclass
98 | class Revision:
99 |     """One version at which the registered primary completion date changed."""
100 |     version: int
101 |     submitted: str          # when the sponsor filed this revision
102 |     pcd: str                # the date as of this version
103 |     pcd_type: str           # ESTIMATED or ACTUAL
104 |     status: str
105 |     moved_days: int | None  # how far the date moved vs the previous version
106 |     held_days: int | None   # days remaining on the OLD date when it was moved
107 | 
108 |     @property
109 |     def is_late_move(self) -> bool:
110 |         """Moved with under 90 days left on a date the sponsor had been showing.
111 |         Not proof of anything on its own; it is the observation the study counts."""
112 |         return self.held_days is not None and 0 <= self.held_days < 90
113 | 
114 |     @property
115 |     def carried_expired(self) -> bool:
116 |         """The date being replaced had ALREADY PASSED when the sponsor replaced it.
117 | 
118 |         A stronger signal than a late move, and a different one. A late revision can
119 |         be a genuine surprise late in a trial. A date left standing after it lapsed
120 |         means the public registry showed a completion date the sponsor had already
121 |         missed, for `-held_days` days, with no correction. Rocket Pharmaceuticals
122 |         carried a 2022-06 date until 2024-04 -- 677 days expired.
123 |         """
124 |         return self.held_days is not None and self.held_days < 0
125 | 
126 |     @property
127 |     def days_expired(self) -> int:
128 |         return -self.held_days if self.carried_expired else 0
129 | 
130 | 
131 | @dataclass
132 | class TrialHistory:
133 |     nct: str
134 |     sponsor: str
135 |     phases: list[str]
136 |     n_versions: int
137 |     revisions: list[Revision]
138 | 
139 |     @property
140 |     def first_pcd(self) -> str | None:
141 |         return self.revisions[0].pcd if self.revisions else None
142 | 
143 |     @property
144 |     def last_pcd(self) -> str | None:
145 |         return self.revisions[-1].pcd if self.revisions else None
146 | 
147 |     @property
148 |     def total_slip_days(self) -> int | None:
149 |         a, b = _parse_date(self.first_pcd), _parse_date(self.last_pcd)
150 |         return (b - a).days if a and b else None
151 | 
152 |     @property
153 |     def n_late_moves(self) -> int:
154 |         return sum(r.is_late_move for r in self.revisions)
155 | 
156 |     @property
157 |     def n_expired_carried(self) -> int:
158 |         return sum(r.carried_expired for r in self.revisions)
159 | 
160 |     @property
161 |     def max_days_expired(self) -> int:
162 |         """Longest stretch this trial showed a completion date that had already passed."""
163 |         return max((r.days_expired for r in self.revisions), default=0)
164 | 
165 |     def as_dict(self) -> dict:
166 |         d = asdict(self)
167 |         d.update(first_pcd=self.first_pcd, last_pcd=self.last_pcd,
168 |                  total_slip_days=self.total_slip_days, n_late_moves=self.n_late_moves)
169 |         return d
170 | 
171 | 
172 | def fetch_history(nct: str) -> TrialHistory:
173 |     """Walk one trial's version history and return only the versions that moved the
174 |     primary completion date."""
175 |     listing = _get(f"{BASE}/{nct}/history")
176 |     changes = listing.get("changes", [])
177 |     if not changes:
178 |         raise ValueError(f"{nct}: no version history returned")
179 | 
180 |     # Version 0 is always fetched (it establishes the first date); after that only
181 |     # versions that touched the status module can have moved it.
182 |     candidates = [c for c in changes
183 |                   if c["version"] == 0 or STATUS_LABEL in (c.get("moduleLabels") or [])]
184 | 
185 |     sponsor, phases = "", []
186 |     revisions: list[Revision] = []
187 |     prev_pcd: date | None = None
188 |     prev_raw = ""
189 | 
190 |     for c in candidates:
191 |         snap = _cached(nct, c["version"])
192 |         proto = snap.get("study", snap).get("protocolSection", {})
193 |         status_mod = proto.get("statusModule", {})
194 |         pcd_struct = status_mod.get("primaryCompletionDateStruct") or {}
195 |         raw = pcd_struct.get("date")
196 |         pcd = _parse_date(raw)
197 |         if pcd is None:
198 |             continue
199 | 
200 |         if not sponsor:
201 |             sponsor = (proto.get("sponsorCollaboratorsModule", {})
202 |                        .get("leadSponsor", {}).get("name", ""))
203 |             phases = proto.get("designModule", {}).get("phases", []) or []
204 | 
205 |         if prev_pcd is not None and pcd == prev_pcd:
206 |             continue  # status module changed for some other reason
207 | 
208 |         submitted = _parse_date(c.get("lastUpdateSubmitQcDate") or c.get("date"))
209 |         moved = (pcd - prev_pcd).days if prev_pcd else None
210 |         # How much runway the OLD date still had when the sponsor moved it. Negative
211 |         # means they moved it after it had already passed, which is its own signal.
212 |         held = (prev_pcd - submitted).days if prev_pcd and submitted else None
213 | 
214 |         revisions.append(Revision(
215 |             version=c["version"],
216 |             submitted=submitted.isoformat() if submitted else "",
217 |             pcd=raw,
218 |             pcd_type=pcd_struct.get("type", ""),
219 |             status=c.get("status", ""),
220 |             moved_days=moved,
221 |             held_days=held,
222 |         ))
223 |         prev_pcd, prev_raw = pcd, raw
224 | 
225 |     return TrialHistory(nct=nct, sponsor=sponsor, phases=phases,
226 |                         n_versions=len(changes), revisions=revisions)
227 | 
228 | 
229 | def demo() -> None:
230 |     """Self-check against a trial whose history is known to be long and messy."""
231 |     h = fetch_history("NCT04613596")
232 |     assert h.n_versions > 50, h.n_versions
233 |     assert h.sponsor, "lead sponsor should resolve"
234 |     assert len(h.revisions) >= 4, f"expected several PCD moves, got {len(h.revisions)}"
235 |     # First recorded date must precede the last, or the walk is out of order.
236 |     assert h.revisions == sorted(h.revisions, key=lambda r: r.version)
237 |     # moved_days is None only on the first revision.
238 |     assert all(r.moved_days is not None for r in h.revisions[1:])
239 | 
240 |     print(f"{h.nct}  {h.sponsor}  {'/'.join(h.phases) or 'n/a'}")
241 |     print(f"  {h.n_versions} versions, {len(h.revisions)} of them moved the date")
242 |     print(f"  {h.first_pcd} -> {h.last_pcd}   total slip {h.total_slip_days} days")
243 |     print(f"  late moves (<90d notice): {h.n_late_moves}")
244 |     print()
245 |     print(f"  {'ver':>4} {'submitted':>11} {'PCD':>11} {'type':>10} {'moved':>7} {'held':>6}")
246 |     for r in h.revisions:
247 |         print(f"  {r.version:>4} {r.submitted:>11} {r.pcd:>11} {r.pcd_type:>10} "
248 |               f"{'' if r.moved_days is None else f'{r.moved_days:+d}':>7} "
249 |               f"{'' if r.held_days is None else r.held_days:>6}"
250 |               f"{'  <- late' if r.is_late_move else ''}")
251 |     print("\nok")
252 | 
253 | 
254 | if __name__ == "__main__":
255 |     demo()
